turns.content: I need to tell somebody. my granddaughter Nizhoni finished her first weaving. all by herself. start to finish
That's wonderful! Completing a full weaving piece is a real accomplishment, especially for someone her age. How does it look? And more importantly, how does she feel about it?

it is small. maybe 8 inches by 10 inches. the edges are a little uneven and the tension is not perfect. but it is hers, you know. she did every single row. she picked the colors herself. red and grey and white. and she is so proud of it she will not stop showing people

That sounds beautiful -- the fact that she chose the colors and saw it through from beginning to end matters so much more than perfect tension at this stage. Is she showing it to everyone who walks through the door?

everyone. she showed Raymond, she showed her mom, she showed two of my patients who were in the waiting room at the wrong time. she held it up and said look what I made. one of my patients told her it was museum quality and she believed it. I did not correct anyone

Ha, I love that -- ambushing patients with her weaving in the waiting room. That confidence is so great to see. And honestly, the fact that she's proud enough to show everyone tells you she's hooked on the craft. That's more valuable than any technical perfection. Are you going to hang it up somewhere?

she wants it on the wall. I told her we would frame it. Raymond said he would make a small silver hook for it. she almost cried when he said that, you know. she tries to act tough but she is still a kid

A silver hook from Raymond -- that makes it a real family collaboration. The weaving, the framing, the handmade hook. That piece is going to mean something to her for the rest of her life. How long did it take her to finish?

about three weeks. she would come over after school and sit at the loom for an hour, sometimes two. some days she got frustrated and wanted to quit. I told her the loom does not care about your mood, you just keep going. she kept going

Three weeks of showing up, even on the frustrating days -- that's discipline and persistence, and she's learning that through the work itself. "The loom doesn't care about your mood" is honestly advice that applies to just about everything in life. Do you think she's going to start a second piece soon?

she already asked me what pattern to do next. I told her to sit with the first one for a while. look at it. see what she would do different. you know there is no rush. the loom will be there. but yes. she will start another one. I can see it in her. the wool has her now

"The wool has her now" -- I think you're right. Once someone gets that feeling of making something with their own hands and seeing it through, it doesn't let go. And your advice to sit with the first piece before rushing to the next one is wise. She'll learn more from studying what she already made than from jumping straight into the next thing. You should be proud of her, and of yourself for teaching her.
I am proud. I do not say it enough probably. but today I am saying it. she did good. ahéhee' for letting me brag a little

You absolutely should brag -- that's what this moment is for. Nizhoni earned it, and so did you. Enjoy that piece on the wall with its silver hook. And when she finishes the next one, you can brag about that too.
